mainEntity.name: Is DCF the only accepted method for FEMA valuation of unlisted companies?

acceptedAnswer.text: In practice, yes. While Rule 21 says “internationally accepted pricing methodology,” RBI and AD banks consistently require DCF for unlisted companies. For listed companies, SEBI pricing guidelines apply and no separate valuation is needed.

mainEntity.name: Who can issue a FEMA valuation certificate?

acceptedAnswer.text: A SEBI-registered Merchant Banker or a Chartered Accountant in practice. As a practicing CA (FCA) and IBBI Registered Valuer, CA V. Viswanathan is qualified to issue FEMA pricing certificates.

mainEntity.name: What happens if shares are issued below FEMA floor price?

acceptedAnswer.text: It constitutes a FEMA contravention. The company and directors face compounding proceedings under Section 15 of FEMA, 1999. Compounding fees can be substantial, and in serious cases, ED investigation may follow.

mainEntity.name: Can the same valuation be used for both FEMA and Income Tax purposes?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not always. FEMA requires DCF-based fair value. Income Tax Rule 11UA accepts multiple methods (NAV, DCF, or comparable transaction). The values may differ. We prepare dual-purpose reports where possible, and flag conflicts where they arise.

mainEntity.name: How long is a FEMA valuation valid?

acceptedAnswer.text: There is no explicit validity period in FEMA regulations. However, AD banks typically expect the valuation to be not more than 6 months old. For rapidly growing companies, a valuation older than 3 months may be questioned.
